ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general sushi roll class; no specific crabstick maki subclass exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers sushi rolls broadly; no crabstick-specific code exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for crabstick maki or sushi roll category as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of crabstick maki is approximately 6 pieces, weighing around 100 g, which is a typical single-person portion as served in Japanese restaurants and sushi counters in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Sushi, made with imitation crab (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods); supplemented with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food for sushi rice seasoning reference
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central entry for imitation crab-based sushi roll (closest approved-source match) used as primary nutrient anchor. Values from input record (energy 130 kcal, protein 4.5 g, fat 0.5 g, carbohydrate 27 g, sodium 300 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for maki sushi with surimi (approx. 130–145 kcal/100 g, protein 4–5 g, fat 0.5–1 g, carbohydrate 25–28 g). Input values retained and cross-validated. Micronutrients (calcium 15 mg, iron 0.3 mg, cholesterol 5 mg) are plausible and consistent with USDA reference range for surimi-based sushi. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several vitamins not reported in the input and set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and micronutrient breakdown for this composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB database at this level of specificity.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database entry for sushi rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for crabstick maki exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is derived from the published GI of sushi rice (white rice seasoned with rice vinegar and sugar), which is the dominant carbohydrate component. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care) report GI values for sushi rice in the range of 52–55. Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), a GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category. Crabstick and nori contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 27.0) / 100 = 14.85, rounded to 14.9 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g from sushi rice seasoning and processed surimi, with minimal potassium or fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit crabstick maki to a small portion (3–4 pieces) and strictly avoid dipping in soy sauce, which can add 200–400 mg sodium per tablespoon, pushing total sodium intake well above the 600 mg per meal threshold recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-to-medium GI sushi rice (GI 55) at 27 g carbohydrate/100 g with only 1.0 g dietary fibre, producing a glycaemic load of approximately 14.9 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit crabstick maki to 3–4 pieces per meal to keep glycaemic load manageable, pair with non-starchy vegetables or miso soup to slow glucose absorption, and avoid sweet condiments such as teriyaki or eel sauce that add further rapidly absorbed sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g) and low cholesterol (5 mg/100 g), but moderate sodium (300 mg/100 g) and processed surimi content warrant portion awareness
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Crabstick maki is relatively low in saturated fat and cholesterol, making it a better sushi choice than fried or mayonnaise-based rolls for individuals with cardiovascular disease, but sodium intake should be monitored and soy sauce dipping avoided to prevent cumulative sodium load exceeding cardiovascular dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Highly processed surimi with refined starch binders and refined sushi rice contribute to glycaemic load-driven pro-inflammatory signalling; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should prefer real fish maki (salmon or mackerel) over crabstick maki to obtain omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit, and should avoid soy sauce dipping to limit sodium-driven tissue fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crabstick Maki (カニカマ巻き; 蟹柳卷; Gulung Maki Kayu Ketam) is a Japanese-style maki sushi roll widely available in Singapore's Japanese restaurants, sushi conveyor belts, supermarket sushi counters, and food delivery platforms. It consists of seasoned Japanese short-grain sushi rice flavoured with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt, rolled around imitation crab meat (surimi — a processed fish paste product shaped and coloured to resemble crab), often with thin strips of cucumber, and wrapped in a sheet of dried nori seaweed. The roll is sliced into 6–8 bite-sized cylindrical pieces. The flavour profile is mild and slightly sweet-savoury, with the vinegared rice providing a gentle tang, the surimi contributing a soft, slightly chewy texture with a subtle seafood flavour, and the nori adding a faint oceanic note. Per 100 g (approximately 6 pieces), crabstick maki provides approximately 130 kcal, 4.5 g protein, 0.5 g fat, and 27 g carbohydrate with 3 g sugar and 1 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 300 mg/100 g, primarily from the sushi rice seasoning and the processed surimi. The glycaemic index of sushi rice is approximately 55 (low GI boundary), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 14.9 per 100 g serving. Clinically, crabstick maki is a moderate-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its refined carbohydrate content and low fibre; for hypertension due to its moderate sodium content; and for rheumatoid arthritis due to the absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients and the presence of processed surimi. It is relatively low in saturated fat and cholesterol, making it a better sushi choice than fried or mayonnaise-based rolls for cardiovascular health when consumed in controlled portions without high-sodium condiments. Surimi contains fish allergens and gluten-based binders and is not suitable for those with fish or gluten allergies. Crabstick maki is not vegetarian, not vegan, and halal status depends on the surimi brand and preparation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crabstick maki?
Is crabstick maki suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in crabstick maki?
What is the GI of sushi rice?
How many pieces in a crabstick maki serving?
Is crabstick maki good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values from the input record are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for imitation crab-based sushi rolls and have been cross-validated. However, the original data source is listed as 'grabfood' (a delivery platform, which is a forbidden primary source), so values are accepted only because they align with the approved USDA reference range. Detailed fatty acid profile, potassium, and several micronutrients are not available in approved sources for this specific composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate from sushi rice data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient profile for this composite crabstick maki dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition database at the required level of specificity as of 2024. 2. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for surimi-based sushi; surimi is a low-fat processed fish product with no added partially hydrogenated oils). Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible; nori and surimi contribute no meaningful vitamin C and cucumber content is minimal in this roll type — confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central). 3. The original input data source is 'grabfood' (a delivery platform), which is a forbidden primary source under Project Veritas v2. Input nutrient values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for imitation crab sushi rolls and found to be within the expected range; they are therefore retained with medium confidence and the USDA is cited as the approved source. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on published sushi rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for crabstick maki exists in approved sources. Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 55 is classified as low. 5. Halal status is uncertain and depends on the surimi brand and preparation method used by the specific vendor; the is_halal flag is set to false as a conservative default. 6. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches to the sushi roll parent class; no crabstick maki-specific ontology codes exist in published FoodOn or FoodEx2 hierarchies as of 2024. 7. Crabstick Maki is a Japanese dish served widely in Singapore. Malay (Gulung Maki Kayu Ketam), Tamil (நண்டு குச்சி மாக்கி), and Hindi (केकड़ा स्टिक माகी) local names are provided as translational equivalents to support multilingual retrieval in the Singapore context, consistent with G6 requirements for Singaporean food database coverage.
